coredata.dc:title: Determination of gold nanoparticles in environmental water samples by second-order optical scattering using dithiotreitol-functionalized CdS quantum dots after cloud point extraction
coredata.prism:issueName: Special Issue on Emerging Contaminants in engineered and natural environment
coredata.dc:creator: Mandyla, Spyridoula P.
Tsogas, George Z.
Vlessidis, Athanasios G.
Giokas, Dimosthenis L.
coredata.dc:description: This work presents a new method for the sensitive and selective determination of gold nanoparticles in water samples. The method combines a sample preparation and enrichment step based on cloud point extraction with a new detection motif that relies on the optical incoherent light scattering of a nano-hybrid assembly that is formed by hydrogen bond interactions between gold nanoparticles and dithiotreitol-functionalized CdS quantum dots. The experimental parameters affecting the extraction and detection of gold nanoparticles were optimized and evaluated to the analysis of gold nanoparticles of variable size and surface coating. The selectivity of the method against gold ions and other nanoparticle species was also evaluated under different conditions reminiscent to those usually found in natural water samples. The developed method was applied to the analysis of gold nanoparticles in natural waters and wastewater with satisfactory results in terms of sensitivity (detection limit at the low pmolL−1 levels), recoveries (>80%) and reproducibility (<9%). Compared to other methods employing molecular spectrometry for metal nanoparticle analysis, the developed method offers improved sensitivity and it is easy-to-operate thus providing an additional tool for the monitoring and the assessment of nanoparticles toxicity and hazards in the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Cloud point extraction
Second-order scattering
Environmental samples

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: Special Issue on Emerging Contaminants in engineered and natural environment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of dithiotreitol-functionalized CdS quantum dots
Characterization and properties of CdS@DTT quantum dots
Method calibration and analytical figures of merit

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the experimental procedure for the extraction of gold nanoparticles from water samples.
Particle size distribution of (a) CdS@DTT quantum dots and (b) CdS@DTT quantum dots—gold nanoparticle assemblies and (c) ATR-IR spectra of CdS@DTT quantum dots.
Effect of (a) CdS@DTT concentration and (b) solution pH on the second-order light scattering of CdS@DTT—AuNP assemblies. Experimental conditions as described in the text.
Response of AuNPs of variable size and surface functionalization on the second-order scattering intensity of CdS@DTT quantum dots.
Recovery rates of AuNPs from fortified water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: DOM=Dissolved organic matter, COD=Chemical oxygen demand.

head.ce:title: Determination of gold nanoparticles in environmental water samples by second-order optical scattering using dithiotreitol-functionalized CdS quantum dots after cloud point extraction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents a new method for the sensitive and selective determination of gold nanoparticles in water samples. The method combines a sample preparation and enrichment step based on cloud point extraction with a new detection motif that relies on the optical incoherent light scattering of a nano-hybrid assembly that is formed by hydrogen bond interactions between gold nanoparticles and dithiotreitol-functionalized CdS quantum dots. The experimental parameters affecting the extraction and detection of gold nanoparticles were optimized and evaluated to the analysis of gold nanoparticles of variable size and surface coating. The selectivity of the method against gold ions and other nanoparticle species was also evaluated under different conditions reminiscent to those usually found in natural water samples. The developed method was applied to the analysis of gold nanoparticles in natural waters and wastewater with satisfactory results in terms of sensitivity (detection limit at the low pmolL−1 levels), recoveries (>80%) and reproducibility (<9%). Compared to other methods employing molecular spectrometry for metal nanoparticle analysis, the developed method offers improved sensitivity and it is easy-to-operate thus providing an additional tool for the monitoring and the assessment of nanoparticles toxicity and hazards in the environment.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, engineered nanoparticles have been the driving force for the development of new technologies in various applications owing to their unique physicochemical and optoelectronic properties. However, as their production and use rises, the environmental levels of nanoparticles are expected to increase mainly due to their occurrence in waste streams and direct emission pathways [1]. As a result, several regulation authorities have classified nanoparticles as emerging contaminants due to their potential environmental and health impacts. Hence, there is an increasing demand for appropriate analytical methods that would enable the efficient determination of nanoparticles in environmental samples.

The development of analytical methods for the determination of nanoparticles in environmental samples typically involves the combination of a sample preparation procedure with an appropriate detection technique. A variety of nanoparticle separation and enrichment methods have been described that involve both instrumental (e.g. field-flow fractionation, chromatography) [2–6] as well as off-line extraction procedures (e.g. liquid–liquid extraction, solid phase extraction, cloud point extraction, liquid or solid phase mixtroextraction etc) [7–12]. Of these methods, cloud point extraction (CPE) has received most attention owing to its simplicity and ease of operation, high enrichment factors, increased extraction efficiency and compatibility with most instrumental detectors [10,13–16]. With regards to identification and quantification of nanoparticles, atomic detectors such as ETAAS and ICP-MS are commonly utilized [3,9–16]. These detectors offer high selectivity and sensitivity and enable the determination of nanoparticles as their precursor metal ions at ultra-trace levels but they are costly and sometimes require sophisticated equipment and complex as well as time-consuming experimental procedures. On the other hand, owing to sensitivity and selectivity barriers the deployment of molecular spectrometry for the trace analysis of nanoparticles is still scarce. Only recently, we and others have demonstrated the potential of molecular spectroscopy for the determination of nanoparticles in environmental samples using molecular emission detectors such as fluorescence and chemiluminescence [17–19]. However, the majority of methods that rely on molecular emission enable the determination of nanoparticle species at relatively high concentration levels (i.e nmolL−1) as compared to the predicted environmental concentrations of nanoparticles in natural waters (i.e femtomolL−1 to picomolL−1) [20]. Therefore, analytical methods that can accomplish the determination of nanoparticles at ultra-trace levels are still in demand.

Scattering of light radiation by a solution containing dispersed particulate matter is a well-known phenomenon in optical detection methods and is used in nephelomteric detectors to measure the scattered radiation at a 90° angle to that of the radiation source. A similar phenomenon occurs in spectrofluorimetric detectors due to diffraction of light from the monochromator grating which passes through the emission monochromator and is detected as a peak at twice the excitation wavelength [21]. This diffraction is called second-order diffraction of scattered excitation light (or second-order scattering–SOS) and it is a non-linear optical scattering phenomenon that has been typically dealt as an unwanted source of light and it was suppressed or eliminated using optical long-pass filters.

The intensity of second-order scattered light, in analogy to nephelometry, is proportional to the concentration of the scattering particles provided that they are sufficiently separated from each other [22,23]. On the basis of this principle several studies have used SOS as an analytical detection strategy after reaction of the target analytes with specific reagents in order to form a suspension of ion-association aggregates which are large enough to scatter the incident light. This approach has been applied to the determination of micro-amounts of therapeutic drugs [24,25], biomacromolecules such as nucleic acids and proteins [26,27], anti-coagulation agents [28], food additives [29] and as an alternative detection strategy for non-fluorescence compounds (e.g. oligomers, saccharides, etc) in liquid chromatography [30]. Recently, scattering of light at double the incident wavelength has been observed in concentrated solutions of organic and inorganic nanoparticles and has been utilized for the investigation of nanoparticle properties in standard solutions [31,32] or as a template for the detection of analytes that can interact with nanoparticles and induce their aggregation [33]. However, the use of SOS for the determination of nanoparticles at ultra-trace levels has not been investigated.

The purpose of this work is to describe the development of a new and expedient analytical methodology for quantification of nanoparticles in aqueous solutions that provides enhanced sensitivity and selectivity compared to previous methods based on molecular spectrometry detectors. Gold nanoparticles which are probably the most widely utilized nanoparticles today [3] were effectively extracted and preconcentrated simultaneously from water samples by means of cloud point extraction. Detection was accomplished using a standard nephelometric experimental setup based on the optical incoherent light scattering, which depends linearly on the concentration of the scatter centers [23]. These centers were generated by the formation of a nano-hybrid assembly between dithiol functionalized CdS quantum dots and gold nanoparticles, and scatter the excitation light at double wavelength. The method was successfully applied to the determination of gold nanoparticles in environmental samples with high sensitivity and good selectivity against other nanoparticle species and metal ions.

All reagents used were of analytical grade. Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (≥99.9% trace metals basis), sodium borohydride, tri-sodium citrate, polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP10, average MW 10,000), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, average MW 22,000), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), DL-dithiotreitol solution (1.0M), sodium sulfide nonahydrate, sodium nitrate and humic acid dry powder were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Triton X-114 (TX-114), EDTA disodium salt, cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate and sodium thiosulphate were procured from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). ZnO nanoparticles dispersion (35–100nm) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. A dispersion of 1mgL−1 was prepared in water under vigorous mixing. Immediately before sampling the mixture was re-suspended under ultrasound irradiation for 5min.

The UV/Vis spectra of nanoparticle solutions were recorded in matched quartz cells of 1cm path length in a Jenway 6405 UV/Vis spectrophotometer. Second-order scattering was measured in a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorimeter with 1cm-thick quartz cuvettes. No low-pass or high-pass optical filters were used to suppress second order satellite lines from the diffraction gratings of the spectrofluorimeter. The size of the nano-hybrid assemblies was measured with a laser diffraction particle size analyzer (Shimadzu SALD-2300) using a 12mL batch cell under stirring.

The experimental procedures for the synthesis of golf nanoparticles were adopted from the literature. Detailed information on the synthetic procedures and characterization can be found in the Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of dithiotreitol-functionalized CdS quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Water soluble dithiotreitol functionalized CdS quantum dots (CdS@DTT QDs) were synthesized by a straightforward one-pot process according to the procedure reported by Cai et al. [34] for the preparation of l-cysteine-capped CdS nanoparticles with some modifications. To 25mL of 0.01M Tris–HCl buffer (pH=7.4) that was argon-saturated and thoroughly degassed, 12mL of 12.5mM Cd(NO3)2 ×4H2O were added under stirring. Then, 20μL of 0.4M Na2S×9H2O were added dropwise into the vortex of the solution and stirred vigorously for 10min. To this mixture, 1mL of 1.0M DDT solution was added and a turbid suspension appeared. The suspension was stirred for 1h at room temperature followed by 10min of flushing with argon to remove the unreacted sulfide. To remove the excess of DDT the suspension was centrifuged at 6000rpm for 30min. The supernatant was discarded and the precipitated CdS@DTT QDs were re-dissolved in doubly distilled water. The washing procedure was repeated 3 times. The CdS@DTT QDs were precipitated again by centrifugation and dried overnight at 45–50°C. The dried material was weighted and re-suspended in an appropriate volume of doubly distilled water under the influence of ultrasound irradiation (10min, 37 KHz) in order to prepare a suspension of 0.435gL−1.

Genuine water samples were used for evaluating the efficiency of the method under realistic conditions. These were collected from a local wastewater treatment plant, Louros river (NW Greece) and Lake Pamvotis (Ioannina, Greece). The samples were filtered through 0.2μm cellulose filters and stored at 4°C until use.

Cloud Point Extraction of nanoparticles. Typically, to 9.0mL of an aqueous standard solution containing 1.5–35.5pmolL−1 of citrate capped gold nanoparticles (as model species) or real water sample, 50μL of 0.2M NaNO3, 0.2mL of thiosulfate (0.25M), 0.5mL of saturated EDTA and 0.3mL of Triton X-114 (5% w/v) were added sequentially with interim mixing. The mixture was vortex agitated for 1min and incubated for 10min in a thermostated water bath at 40°C. Separation of the micellar phase from the bulk aqueous phase was accomplished by centrifugation at 6000rpm for 10min. The vials were then cooled in an ice-bath in order to increase the viscosity of the surfactant rich phase and the supernatant was descanted by inverting the vial. The condensed micellar phase containing the nanoparticles was reconstituted with 0.5mL of 10mM H2O2 for 20min. A second CPE procedure was repeated by centrifuging the cloudy solution without the addition of reagents (i.e. without TX-114, masking agents, ionic strength and pH regulators) by incubating the sample in open vials for 10min at 40°C in order to promote phase separation and decompose the remaining amount of H2O2. The supernatant solution from the second CPE was discarded and the micellar phase, containing the AuNPs, was treated with 1.5mL of methanol, 0.5mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH=7.0) and 0.2mL of CdS@DTT quantum dots. After 45min, the concentration of AuNPs was determined by recording the optical intensity at 600nm after radiating the sample at 300nm (incident wavelength). The net SOS signal intensity was calculated by subtracting the SOS intensity of the samples from the SOS intensity of the blank. The overall experimental procedure is graphically demonstrated in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and properties of CdS@DTT quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Upon dissolution in water the synthesized CdS@DTT QDs formed a turbid solution which was an indication that the QDs aggregated into larger structures. Light scattering experiments in the CdS@DTT QDs suspensions verified the formation of large aggregate assemblies with an average diameter of approximately 0.70μm and a modal value (i.e. the most frequent value) of 0.56μm (Fig. 2 a). Similar motifs have also been observed in previous studies reporting on the room-temperature synthesis of thiol-functionalized CdS quantum dots [34–36]. According to these studies, the disappearance of S-H stretch bands of thiols is a strong indication that thiols adsorbed on the nanocrystal surface through covalent bonds [34,36]. Hence, they reasoned that aggregation is essentially driven by non-covalent interactions between QDs, due to the OHOH hydrogen bonding of the exposed hydrophilic hydroxyl groups of thiols [36]. The ATR-IR spectra of the CdS@DTT QDs depicted in Fig. 2c shows no vibration bands at 2550–2670cm−1, which indicates the lack of free SH groups. Therefore, we concluded that both SH functional groups have been adsorbed on the nanocrystals surface and that aggregation was driven by hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of DTT.

The intensity of second order scattering of CdS@DTT QDs in the presence of AuNPs exhibited a linear decrease with increasing nanoparticle concentration. In order to explain this observation we measured the average size of the CdS@DTT QDs in the presence of AuNPs. As a compromise to instrument sensitivity we used a concentrated solution composed of 1.5mL of 17nm citrate-capped AuNPs solution and 10mL of CdS@DTT QDs and recorded the size distribution of the CdS@DTT QDs-AuNPs assembly. According to the size distribution depicted in Fig. 2b, the average size of the aggregates increased from 0.70μm, in the absence of AuNPs, to 1.63μm in the presence of AuNPs (modal value 0.89μm). As the size of the particles increased, the angular distribution of scattered light became asymmetrical and increased in the forward direction due to mutually destructive backscattering effects [37], and decreased at 90° angle [38]. Therefore, SOS intensity decreased with increasing particle size.

To elucidate the validity of these observations in dilute solutions we compared the UV/Vis absorption spectra of dilute AuNPs solutions in phosphate buffer solution (pH=7) to that obtained under the same experimental conditions in the presence of 50mgL−1 CdS@DTT QDs. No red or blue shift in the maximum absorbance of AuNPs was observed in the presence of QDs which indicated that AuNPs did not aggregate but they were incorporated within the QDs aggregate assembly.

Taking these findings into consideration, we assumed that AuNPs bonded to the QDs aggregate through hydrogen bonds and acted as bridges between QDs, enhancing their aggregation state and increasing their size which in turn leads to a quenching in the SOS intensity at 90° angle.

Firstly, the optimum detection conditions were investigated by studying the variables that affect the light scattering properties of CdS@DTT QDs upon interaction with AuNPs. The influence of pH, QDs concentration, incubation time and temperature were investigated by adding AuNPs@CA (71.6 pM) directly into 2.5mL of aqueous methanolic solution (60:40 MeOH:H2O) containing 50mgL−1 of CdS@DTT QDs. We used a hydro-methanolic mixture instead of pure aqueous solutions because water could re-constitute the micellar phase collected after CPE and interfere with the subsequent detection. Moreover, methanol could lower the dielectric constant of the mixture and favor hydrogen bond interactions between the hydroxyl groups of CdS@DTT QDs and the molecular coating of nanoparticles. To decide on the optimum MeOH concentration, we compared the net SOS signal intensity in the presence and absence of AuNPs under variable methanol concentrations. Increasing concentrations of MeOH up to 60% had no effect on SOS intensity, while further increase induced a small decline (∼5%) compared to that obtained in the absence of methanol. To ensure complete dissolution of the condensed micellar phase collected after CPE without impairing sensitivity 60% MeOH was selected as optimum.

The response of the SOS intensity to increasing CdS concentration was evaluated using increasing amounts of QDs in order to obtain the maximum (net) signal. The graph of Fig. 3 a shows that the scattering intensity increased abruptly at 35mgL−1 and remained quite stable up to 70mgL−1 declining again at higher QD concentrations (>90mgL−1). Therefore 35mgL−1 of CdS@DTT QDs were used throughout the measurements.

The influence of pH was investigated using different buffers in the pH range of 2–11 (Fig. 3b). Neutral pH conditions (pH=7–8) produced the higher net signal possibly because they favored the formation of hydrogen bonds between the surface coating of AuNPs (i.e. citrate) and the hydroxyl groups of DTT. On the other hand, acidic (pH<4) and alkaline solutions (pH>9) had a negative effect on SOS intensity. This observation may be explained by the instability of CdS QDs at strongly acidic and alkaline solutions [39] as well as by the fact that too acidic or alkaline conditions do not favor the formation of hydrogen bonds. Therefore, a citric acid-Na2HPO4 buffer solution (pH=7.0) was used for regulating the pH of the final solution.

The incubation time of CdS@DTT QDs with AuNPs was investigated in the time period from 0 to 60min at 5min intervals. According to the SOS intensity profile the net signal reached its maximum value after 45min, gradually declining thereafter possibly due to intense aggregation. Therefore, SOS measurements were undertaken 45min after the introduction of QDs.

Under these conditions (35mgL−1 QDs, pH=7 and incubation for 45min) we evaluated the SOS intensity, in the presence of AuNPs of different size and surface functionalization in order to elucidate whether the method is suited to gain a sum parameter, i.e. the total concentration of nanosized Au in the sample. This is of outmost importance since nanoparticles in real samples exist in a large variety of sizes and coatings. The results gathered in Table 1 shows that the net SOS intensity (SOS intensity of the blank minus the SOS intensity of the sample) increased with increasing AuNP concentration but the signal intensity varied for AuNPs of different size and coating. In the formation of nano-hydrib assemblies between different nanomaterials the influence of size and coating is not unexpected because it affects the nature of this interaction For example, Valcarcel and co-workers observed that PVP coating can completely block the access of thiol-functionalized carbon dots on the surface of AgNPs [19]. Accordingly, smaller nanoparticles could conceivably be more reactive due to their larger surface area and small volume. However, there is still a dearth of information regarding the signal response of molecular spectrometry methods as a function of nanoparticle size [17,19,40]. Based on the results of this study it can be inferred that the method can be used to obtain an estimate of the total concentration of AuNPs by extrapolating the measured signal to that obtained during calibration using AuNPs of specific size and coating (i.e. as equivalents to 17nm citrate-capped AuNPs). This approach is typically employed in all generic analytical methods that measure a total or sum parameter (e.g. assays for total antioxidant capacity as gallic acid equivalents, methods of total phenolic content as gallic acid or trolox equivalents, assays of total thiols as cysteine equivalents, etc) [41,42].

CPE has been shown to be an effective method for the extraction of a variety of nanoparticle species from aqueous solutions including AuNPs [10,13–16,18]. However, different experimental conditions have been proposed for each species depending also on the detection technique and its compatibility with surfactant media. For example, ICP-MS and CL detection are negatively affected by the presence of the surfactant [10,18], while ETAAS exhibits better tolerance [13,14]. On this basis, we optimized the CPE conditions that enable the efficient coupling of CPE and SOS detection using citrate capped AuNPs. All experiments were also repeated with PVP coated AuNPs in order to ensure that both charged and uncharged species could be effectively determined. The initial experimental conditions for the optimization study were: 0.25% (w/v) TX-114, pH=5.0 and 0.5mM NaNO3, heating at 40°C for 10min, and adjusted separately during the investigation of each parameter.

The extraction of nanoparticles into a micellar medium has been shown to be affected by their surface charge [43]. Specifically, electrostatically stabilized NPs have been extracted at pH values close to their zero point of charge (pHzpc) where Coulombic interactions reach their minimum value while uncharged NPs show less pH-dependence [10,13,14,18,43]. In our experiments, the extraction of AuNPs was accomplished in circumneutral pH values (pH=6.0–7.0). This was primarily attributed to the regulation of the ionic strength of the solution with NaNO3 that could effectively counterbalance the negative repulsion forces among nanoparticles, rendering the influence of pH less important [18,43]. At alkaline conditions the citrate ions on the surface of AuNPs were negatively charged thus deterring their efficient portioning into the hydrophobic micellar core, while at acidic pH values the AuNPs aggregated as evidenced by the change in the color of the solutions (red to blue). In the presence of such aggregates the net SOS signal intensity decreased as compared to the signal recorded with non-aggregated AuNPs which suggests that the formation of hydrogen bond assemblies between the CdS@DTT QDs and AuNP aggregates was not favored. The influence of pH was further verified by the fact that the pH of the solution (over the pH range of 4–9) had a very small influence of the extraction yield of polymer stabilized AuNPs (AuNPs@PVP), which have no charged groups.

The addition of salts is a common practice in micelle mediated extraction of nanoparticles because it contributes to micelle salting out, thus enhancing phase separation, and, as previously discussed, counterbalances the Coulombic repulsion forces among charged nanoparticles favoring their extraction into the micellar medium. To investigate the influence of ionic strength, NaNO3 was selected as an ionic strength regulator because nitrate salts do not affect the cloud point temperature of TX-114. According to our results, NaNO3 concentrations up to 1.0mM enhanced the extraction efficiency of nanoparticles while at higher NaNO3 concentrations the net signal initially decreased and plateued thereafter. This could be attributed to the fact that when the surfactant rich phase was diluted with methanol, the micelles became dissociated and AuNPs aggregated due to the presence of relatively high salt concentrations [43], which obstructed the subsequent association of AuNPs with CdS@DTT QDs. The lack of influence of ionic strength regulation on the apparent extraction efficiency of AuNPs@PVP provided additional evidence that NaNO3 was necessary to screen the surface charge of AuNPs@CA and enhance their extraction into the micellar medium

Experiments with different concentrations of TX-114 showed that maximum extraction efficiency is accomplished at 0.15% (w/v). Lower amounts were insufficient to quantitatively extract the nanoparticles while at higher mounts a reduction in the net signal intensity was observed. On the basis of these findings, 0.15% (w/v) TX-114 was used for extraction.

Other parameters typically affecting the performance of CPE such as incubation time, temperature and centrifugation time were also investigated. The net signal intensity reached a plateau after 10min of incubation while no further gains were attained at temperatures higher than 40°C. Finally, no influence in the extraction yield of AuNPs was observed for centrifugation times higher than 10min at maximum rotational speed (6000rpm). Therefore, extractions were carried out at 40°C for 10min followed by 10min of centrifugation at 6000rpm.

A major challenge for each analytical method developed to quantify metal nanoparticles is the separation of nanoparticles and ionic species of the same metal ion, likely to co-exist in real samples. Therefore, we examined the influence of Au3+ ions on the net SOS signal intensity by extracting 10mL of aqueous solutions containing 10.0μgL−1 of Au3+. The concentration of 10.0μgL−1 was selected because it is well above the usual levels of dissolved Au3+ in natural waters and wastewater (1–80ngL−1 Au3+) [44,45]. The net SOS signal intensity was the same as the blank sample (aqueous solutions devoid of Au3+) which means that the interference of 10.0μgL−1 of dissolved Au3+ was negligible.

Although direct extraction of ionic Au3+ pose as no threat to the analysis, previous studies have suggested that co-extraction of metal ions in the micellar phase may be facilitated by co-adsorption on the surface of nanoparticles or co-extraction with dissolved organic matter [7,13,14]. Since dissolved organic matter is abundant in most natural waters, especially those that are most likely to contain gold nanoparticles (e.g. wastewater, industrial effluent, or sewage), we simulated the influence of dissolved organic matter on the extraction process. More specifically, we prepared standard solutions of humic acid (HA) in the concentration range of 0–15mgL−1 and evaluated its influence on the extraction of 10.0μgL−1 Au3+ species. The net SOS signal intensity in the presence and absence of HA varied by approximately 3.0±0.8% which lied within the experimental error. This indicates that a very small portion of metal ions was co-extracted. This may attributed to the fact that extraction was carried out at circumneutral pH which does not favor the extraction of HA via hydrophobic interactions because HA contains a large number of ionizable groups which are de-protonated at circumneutral pH values [46].

Co-extraction of Au3+ ions through adsorption on the surface of their respective nanoparticle species was also investigated by extracting mixtures of AuNPs and Au3+ (18.0pmolL−1 AuNPs and 10.0μgL−1 Au3+). The results obtained showed an insignificant variation in the net SOS signal intensity (∼1.5–2.0%) compared to that recorded in the absence of ionic species. However, co-extraction of Au3+ increased noticeably (up to 15%) in the presence of 15.0mgL−1 HA. This may be explained by the fact that HA can associate with metal nanoparticles [11]. Therefore, at high concentration levels, HA could complex ionic Au3+ and then co-extracted with AuNPs into the micellar core thus increasing the apparent extraction yield of AuNPs.

To mitigate the potential interference of ionic metal species we examined thiosulfate (TS) and EDTA as masking agents because they have been successfully employed before for separating Au3+ species from AuNPs [13,14,18]. Both TS and EDTA were found to be efficient in overcoming the interference of Au3+ ions in distilled water. However, in genuine water samples the best results were obtained when both masking agents were added in the sample solution. Experiments with real water samples fortified with 18.0pmolL−1 AuNPs and 10.0μgL−1 of Au3+ in the presence of 15.0mgL−1 HA showed that 5.0mM TS and 0.5mL of saturated EDTA, when added before TX-114, could completely alleviate the interference of ionic metal species on the determination of nanoparticles.

The potential inference from other metal nanoparticles was finally investigated using citrate-capped AgNPs and ZnO. The interference of metal oxides at a concentration up to 2 orders of magnitude higher than AuNPs was less than 10%. This may be attributed to the lack of active moieties on their surface that could interfere with the hydrogen bond formation process between citrate-capped AuNPs and CdS@DTT QDs during the detection step. On the other hand, citrate-capped AgNPs could interfere with detection even at equi-molar concentrations to that of AuNPs. Since AgNPs are also widely used in many applications as AuNPs [3], special attention was paid to mitigate their interference. To this end, the micellar phase obtained after extraction was treated with H2O2 in order to dissociate the AgNPs to Ag+ ions and CPE was repeated again without the addition of TX-114 by simply heating the solution, following the procedure described in our previous work [18]. In this manner AuNPs were entrapped again into the micellar phase while the supernatant, containing the dissolved Ag+ ions was discarded. By this procedure no interference from AgNPs was observed even at concentrations 3 orders of magnitude higher than that of AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method calibration and analytical figures of merit
ce:section.ce:para: Method calibration was performed by extracting aqueous standard solutions containing increasing AuNP concentrations. In this manner not only unrecognized variation in the enrichment procedure could be compensated but also the physico-chemical properties of the samples and the standards were the same. Under the optimized conditions, linearity of the calibration function was good (R2 =0.9984) for AuNPs@citrate (17nm) concentrations in the range of 1.5–35.5pmolL−1 (y=0.15x+4.71). The linear range of calibration is relatively narrow but it is better than previous methods for nanoparticle analysis which rely on molecular spectrometry detectors [17,18,40]. The detection limit, defined as three times the signal to noise ratio, was 0.58pmolL−1 by extracting only 10mL of water samples which lied within the predicted concentration ranges for the examined nanoparticles in environmental samples [3,20]. Obviously, the sensitivity of the method is inferior to that of atomic detectors (ETAAS and ICP) [5,9–14] but the use of these detectors also entails higher costs, sophisticated equipment and trained operators while the developed procedure is simpler and more-cost effective. Moreover, additional improvement in the LOD is feasible by increasing the ratio of sample to extractant volume either by preconcentrating larger sample volumes or by diluting the final extract to a smaller volume. Although higher ratios were not checked it is assumed that even higher enrichment factors could be reached by this approach.

Finally, the precision of the method was calculated as the relative standard deviations of the net SOS signal intensity obtained by extracting 10mL aqueous standard solutions containing 18.0pmolL−1 of AuNPs for three consecutive days and for four independent replicates (n=12). The intraday precision (n=4) was found to be 8.0%, and the interday precision (n=3) was 9.3% which were deemed as satisfactory.

To verify the potential of the developed method for the analysis of real water samples, recovery experiments were conducted in three genuine water samples enriched with different AuNPs@citrate (17nm) concentrations. Two sets of samples (three replicates each) were prepared at each spiking level. The relative recoveries ranged from 78.0 to 109.9% (Table 2 ) which were considered as satisfactory and lied within the range of values reported in previous methods [8,9,11,12,14,18]. These findings suggest that the method is suited for the realistic determination of AuNPs in environmental samples.

In this work, a novel method combining CPE with SOS detection has been developed for the analysis of AuNPs in environmental waters. The method can simultaneously preconcentrate AuNPs of different size and surface functionalization thus providing an estimate of the total concentration of AuNPs in water samples. The developed procedure affords good tolerance to dissolved Au ions, other metal nanoparticles and dissolved organic matter which is likely to be encountered in water samples. A series of natural waters of variable matrix complexity were analyzed with satisfactory analytical features in terms of recovery rates and sensitivity suggesting that the method can be used to assess the hazards and potential toxicity associated with the release and occurrence of AuNPs in environmental samples. Importantly, a new detection strategy relying on the formation of nano-hybrid assemblies between AuNPs and semiconductor nanocrystals has been developed. This detection approach holds promise to the determination and speciation analysis of other metal nanoparticles by appropriately regulating the detection conditions or by designing appropriate chemical probes. Further experiments will include strategies towards this direction.

This work describes a new analytical methodology for the enrichment and determination of gold nanoparticles in environmental samples. In the developed method, gold nanoparticles were initially extracted and by means of cloud point extraction. Detection was accomplished using a standard nephelometric experimental setup based on the optical incoherent light scattering of nano-hybrid assemblies formed between gold nanoparticles and dithiol-functionalized CdS quantum dots.

On the basis of this principle, it was shown that the method could efficiently extract and preconcentrate gold nanoparticles of different size and surface functionalization at the low pico-molar levels from a variety of water samples generally representative of environmental waters.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2016.03.039.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Potential scenarios for nanomaterials release and subsequent alteration in the environment

Analytical chemistry of metallic nanoparticles in natural environments
Toward full spectrum speciation of silver nanoparticles and ionic silver by on-line coupling of hollow fiber flow field-flow fractionation and minicolumn concentration with multiple detectors
Fast separation, characterization, and speciation of gold and silver nanoparticles and their ionic counterparts with micellar electrokinetic chromatography coupled to ICP-MS
Rapid chromatographic separation of dissoluble Ag(I) and silver-Containing nanoparticles of 1–100 nanometer in antibacterial products and environmental waters
Efficient hydrophobization and solvent microextraction for determination of trace nano-sized silver and titanium dioxide in natural waters
Effective and selective extraction of noble metal nanoparticles from environmental water through a non-covalent reversible reaction on an ionic exchange resin
Ligand-assisted extraction for separation and preconcentration of gold nanoparticles from waters
Cloud point extraction as an advantageous preconcentration approach for analysis of trace silver nanoparticles in environmental waters
A novel strategy for sequential analysis of gold nanoparticles and gold ions in water samples by combining magnetic solid phase extraction with inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry
In-situ suspended aggregate microextraction of gold nanoparticles from water samples and determination by electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry
Ultra-trace determination of silver nanoparticles in water samples using cloud point extraction and ETAAS
Species selective preconcentration and quantification of gold nanoparticles using cloud point extraction and electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry
Speciation analysis of silver nanoparticles and silver ions in antibacterial products and environmental waters via cloud point extraction-based separation
Quantification of the uptake of silver nanoparticles and ions to HepG2 cells
Functionalized carbon dots as sensors for gold nanoparticles in spiked samples: formation of nanohybrids
Ultratrace determination of silver gold, and iron oxide nanoparticles by micelle mediated preconcentration/selective back-extraction coupled with flow injection chemiluminescence detection
Reusable sensor based on functionalized carbon dots for the detection of silver nanoparticles in cosmetics via inner filter effect
Modeled environmental concentrations of engineered nanomaterials (TiO2, ZnO Ag, CNT, Fullerenes) for different regions

Resonance Rayleigh scattering, second-order scattering and frequency doubling scattering spectra of Copper(II)-flutamide system with anionic surfactants and its analytical application

Resonance Rayleigh scattering, second-order scattering and frequency doubling scattering methods for the indirect determination of penicillin antibiotics based on the formation of Fe3[Fe(CN)6]2 nanoparticles
Resonance double frequency light scattering of the morin–nucleic acid–cetyltrimethylammonium bromide system and its analytical application
Frequency doubling scattering and second-order scattering spectra of phosphato-molybdate heteroply acid–protein system and their analytical application
Resonance Rayleigh scattering, frequency doubling scattering and second-order scattering spectra of the heparin–crystal violet system and their analytical application
Resonance Rayleigh scattering, second-order scattering and frequency doubling scattering spectra for studying the interaction of erythrosine with Fe(phen)3 2+ and its analytical application

A study on the sizes and concentrations of gold nanoparticles by spectra of absorption, resonance Rayleigh scattering and resonance non-linear scattering
Quantification of fullerene nanoparticles suspensions in water based on optical scattering
Derivatized silver nanoparticles as sensor for ultra-trace nitrate determination based on light scattering phenomenon
